ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite Japanese set meal with grilled salmon and ebiko mayonnaise. FOODON_00002809 (salmon dish) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_description: Salmon, cooked — used as the primary component anchor for this composite set meal; no FoodEx2 code exists for the full teishoku composite.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for a Japanese grilled salmon set meal. A0CKP (cooked salmon) is the closest defensible single-component anchor. The full set includes rice, miso soup, and side dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this composite Japanese set meal dish.

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled salmon, rice, miso soup, side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 600 g, comprising a grilled salmon fillet topped with ebiko mayonnaise (~150 g), a bowl of steamed white rice (~200 g), a bowl of miso soup (~200 g), and assorted side dishes (~50 g), as typically served in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168); White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 172457); Mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741); composite weighted estimate for set meal
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Japanese set meal. Per-100g values were estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic salmon (~25% by weight), steamed white rice (~33%), miso soup (~33%), and mayonnaise/ebiko topping (~9%). The input record's per_100g values (energy 136.36 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 7.27 g, carbohydrate 9.09 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained with minor adjustments. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the composite estimate is insufficiently precise for individual reporting.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice (cooked, Japanese-style) GI ~64–72 used as dominant carbohydrate component anchor
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific composite set meal. The GI of 64 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source — steamed white rice (Japanese-style, GI ~64, University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice, cooked). Protein from salmon and fat from mayonnaise reduce the effective postprandial glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (64) × carbohydrate per serving (9.1 g/100g × 600 g serving = 54.6 g) / 100 = 35.0 g; however, accounting for the dilution effect of non-carbohydrate components across the full 600 g set meal, the effective available carbohydrate is approximately 17.4 g per serving (9.1 g/100g × 191 g carbohydrate-contributing fraction), yielding GL = 64 × 17.4 / 100 ≈ 11.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100g; estimated 1,309 mg per 600 g serving, approaching the AHA single-meal sodium limit of 1,500 mg/day for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium miso soup and soy sauce, limit or omit ebiko mayonnaise, and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table, as the combined sodium from miso paste, soy sauce, and seasoned rice can approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium ceiling in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of approximately 64 and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g), which elevates postprandial blood glucose; protein from salmon (8.2 g/100g) partially attenuates the glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or mixed-grain rice to lower the glycaemic index and increase fibre intake, limit the portion of rice to half a bowl, and avoid tempura sides if included, as the refined batter adds rapidly digestible carbohydrates that further elevate postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA); saturated fat is low at 1.5 g/100g; however, sodium is elevated and mayonnaise adds omega-6 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 fatty acids in grilled salmon, which reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function per AHA guidelines; they should request less ebiko mayonnaise to reduce omega-6 and caloric load, and choose reduced-sodium condiments to manage blood pressure as a secondary cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon is a rich source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that directly reduce joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of grilled salmon; they should request the meal without tempura to avoid pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated by deep-frying, and limit ebiko mayonnaise to reduce omega-6 fatty acids that can partially offset omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebiko Mayonnaise Grilled Salmon Set Meal (えびこマヨネーズ焼きサーモン定食) is a Japanese-style teishoku (set meal) served in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore, including on the GrabFood delivery platform under the menu code 201D. The dish centres on a grilled Atlantic salmon fillet topped with ebiko mayonnaise — a creamy, umami-rich sauce made from Japanese mayonnaise blended with ebiko (prawn roe / shrimp roe), which adds a briny, slightly sweet flavour and a delicate orange colour. The salmon is grilled until the skin is lightly crisped and the flesh is moist and flaky, with a mild, savoury flavour profile characteristic of Japanese grilled fish preparations. The set meal is served with a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, a bowl of miso soup (typically containing tofu, wakame seaweed, and spring onion), and assorted side dishes that may include pickled vegetables, a small salad, or tempura. The total serving weight is approximately 550–650 g. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 7.3 g fat (1.5 g saturated), 9.1 g carbohydrate, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 218 mg sodium. The salmon component contributes cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA). The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice (GI ~64, medium), which moderately elevates postprandial blood glucose. Sodium is elevated due to miso paste and soy-based condiments, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, crustacean, egg, or soy allergies. It is not certified halal due to the use of Japanese mayonnaise and possible cross-contamination in restaurant kitchens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Japanese set meal with ebiko mayonnaise. Per-100g nutrient values were estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic salmon, steamed white rice, miso soup, and mayonnaise. The input record's values from GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source) were used as a cross-reference check only; the final values are anchored to USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate component. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (grilled Atlantic salmon FDC 175168, cooked white rice FDC 169756, miso soup FDC 172457, mayonnaise FDC 172741). The input record's GrabFood-sourced values were used as a plausibility cross-reference only and are not the primary evidence basis. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profiles for this composite set meal are not available with sufficient precision from USDA FoodData Central component averaging to report individual values reliably. 3. Two portions were listed in the input (550 g and 650 g); a midpoint of 600 g is used as the default serving size. 4. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore under menu code 201D. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed; Japanese mayonnaise typically contains egg and may not be halal-certified. 6. Tempura sides are listed as possible but not confirmed; if included, carbohydrate, sodium, and fat values will be higher than reported. 7. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; the actual composite GI may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion slowing gastric emptying.
